Fern Polrrrcel PRACTTcEs C oMMrssroN
428 J Street o Suire 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
December 15,2010
Jennifer K. McCain
Ass istant City Attorney
City of Escondido
201 North Broadway
Escondido, California 92026
RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. ,4.-10-177
Dear Ms. McCain:
This letter responds to yoLlr request for advice on behalf of Escondido City Mayor Sam
Adbed regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") I and
is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does
not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.)
Additionally, we base our advice solely on the provisions of the Act and do not adclress the
applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws, such as common law conflict of interest or
Government Code Section 1090.
QUESTION
1. May Mayor Abed participate in the city council and/or Community Development
Commission's consideration of a governmental decision to acquire land and constrlrct a minor
league ballpark?
CONCLUSION
1. Mayor Abed is presumed not to have a conflict of interest in the governmental
decision because his real property is located more than 500 feet from the property that is the
subject of the govemmental decision. However, this presumption may be rebutted if there are
factors that indicate there will be a material financial effect on Mayor Abed's property, as
discussed below.
' The Political Retbrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections ttl000 rhrough 91014. All statutory
ret'erences are to the GovernmentC<lde, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political practices
Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through 1U997 of Title 2 of the Califirrnia Cgde of Regulati.ns. All
regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Calitbrnia Code <tf Regulations. unless gtherwise indicated.


File No. A-10-177
Page No. 2
FACTS
Mayor Abed owns real property located at 540 and 562 W. Grand Avenue. Mayor Abed
receives rental income from the property located at 562 W. Grand Avenue. He leases tire
property to Tom and Judy Fitzgerald. Tom and Judy Fitzgerald own CrossFit Xystum LLC2 and
have two years remaining on their lease. Tom and Judy Fitzgerald pay $3,100 per month to
Mayor Abed. The property at 540 W. Grand is vacant.
The governmental decision facing the Escondido City Council involves acquiring land
and constntcting a minor league ballpark. Specifically, the City will be acquiring two parcels of
land and using a portion of its own property to constnrct a minor league baflpark that will be
leased to a minor league baseball team. The parcels of property ar" adiocerf to each other and
consist of the "Spruce Street property," located at 480 North Spruce Street consisting of 3.7g
acres; the North County Transit District property, consisting of 1.68 acres; and the eiisting City
public works yard consisting of 7.82 acres. The ballpark will be an open-air, natural gru5, state-
of-the-art Minor League Baseball facility with a seating capacity of approximately 9,000. It is
anticipated that the project will be developed by the Escondido Community Deveiopment
Commission as a redevelopment project. Councilmembers also serve as member of the
Community Development Commission.
City staff has prepared a map showing the location of Mayor Abed's properties in
relation to the "Spruce Street Property" as described. It appears from the map that Mayor
Abed's properties located at 540 and 562 W. Grand Avenue are located well tver 500 feet from
the "Spruce Street Property," at approximately 1,250 feet and 1,270 feet, respectively.
The city council has not yet made any formal decisions regarding acquiring the land and
constructing the ballpark. Mayor Abed seeks advice regarding whether he may participate in the
variotts decisions to acquire the land, constructing and financing the park, as well as the various
land use entitlements that will be required for the project. Mayor Ab;d also seeks advice
regarding what procedures must be followed if he is legally required to abstain from participating
in any governmental decisions regarding the ballpark.
PornNu.ll CoNFLICT oF INTEREST
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have sr.rpported them." (Section 81001(b).) Section 87100
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.
The Commission has adopted an eighrstep standard analysis for deciding whether an
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule,
r CrossFit Xystum is a fitness training center (see wvr..rv.crr-rssfitrlstum.com)


File No. A-10-177
Page No. 3
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a govemmental
decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial "ff.;i;;;;;; 
ior" of his or her
financial interests.
srrps I & 22 Is Mevon ABED A Punr,tc orrrcrel Maxmc, penrrcrparrNc rN M.lxrNc, oR
INrtunNcrNG A GovERNMENTAL DscrsroN?
As a mayot/":*ryllity development commission member, Mayor Abed is a public official
under the Act' (section 82048.) Conse_quently, he may not make, participate in making, or
otherwise use his official position to influenc. ony decisions that will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on any of his economic interests. Because Mayor Abed will
be called upon to consider proposed projects bef9ry the city council, he will be making,
participating in making, or otherwise using his official position to influence a governmental
decision.3
srrr 3: Dors Mlvon AnBo Heva A PoTENTTALLy Drsquar,rryrNc EcoNoMrc lNrenssr?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section
87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her
immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests, including:
o An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation lg703.l(a));
or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any
position of management. (secrion g7103(d); Reguration rgzo3.rlul.i
o An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 0r more. (section bzto:1u;; Regulation 1g703.2.)
o An economic interest in any sollrce of income, including promised income,
aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the-decisiorr. (S""iiln
87 103(c); Regularion 19703.3.)
o An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to
$420 or more.y^i1hi.n r2 months prior to the decision. (Section s"zro:rEi; 
-
Regularion 18703.4.)
I If a public otficial's offlce is listed in Section 87200 ("87zwfilers" include city councilmembers and
planning commission members) and he or she has a conf'lict of interest in a decision noticea at a public meeting,
then he or she must: ( I ) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identity each type of economic
interest involved in the decisio, ot *ill as details of the economic interest, as discussed in regulation
18702'5(b)(l)(B)' on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself;and (3) Ieave the room for rhe durarion
of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent c.alendars, absences and speaking as a
memberofthepublicregardingpersonal interests,special rulesttlundinregulation lgT02.5,subdivisions(c)and(d)
apply. (Section 87105.)


File No. A-10-177
Page No. 4
o An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family - this is the "peisonal financial effects" mle. (Section g7103;
Regulation 18703.5.)
You have indica-ted that Mayor Abed has a real property economic interest in two
properties located at 540 and 562 w. Grand Avenue r"rp..iiu"iy.ir;;;u" orro provided
information regarding Mayor Abed's source-of-ir"o-. 
".onomic interest from his tenants of the
one commercial rental property. Additionally, Mayor Abed has an economic interest in his
business entity that rents the property.
SrBp 4: Is THr EcoNoprrc INrBRrsr Dmrcrr,y oR INDTRECTLy INvor,vro rN THE
GovrnNnrENTAL DncrsroN?s
"In order to determine if a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on a given economic interesiis material, it must first be determined if the official,s
economic interest is directly involved or indirectly involved in the governmental decision.,,
(Regulation 1870a(a).) For governmental decisions that affect."ur"prop",ty interests, the
standards set forrh in regulation rg7o4.2 apply. lRegulation rgl}a@)e).\
Real Prooertv:
Regulation 18704.2(a) states, in pertinent part:
"(a) Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is
directly involved in a govemmental dlcision if any of the rouowin! afpty:
"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of
that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the property [that] is the subject of the governmental decision.,,
You have indicated that the two properties owed by Mayor Abed's are located 1,250 and
7'270 feet from the ball-park site. Therefori, because the site is more than 500 feet from the two
properties, Mayor Abed's real property is indirectly involved in the gor"*-"rtal decisions.
For governmental decisions that affect business entities and sources of income, the
standards set forth in regularion rg7o4.r apply. (Regulation lg70a(aXl).)
a The Statement of Economic Interests you provided indicates that the properties each have a value in
excess of $2,000.
5If a public otficial's economic interest in not directly involved in a governmental decision, it is considered
"indirectly invol ved." (Regulation I g704(a). )


File No. A-10-177
Page No.5
"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of income, and sources of gifts, is
directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when that person, either
directly or by an agent: ( I ) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be
made by firing an application, craim, appeal, or siirilar request or; (z) Is a named
party in, or is the subject of, the p.oc".ding concerning the decision before the
official or the official's agency. A person iJ the subjeciof a proceeding ii a decision
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or"revocation of any license, permit,
or other entitrement to, or contracf with, the subject person.
"(b) If a business entity, source of income, or source of a gift is directly involved in a
governmental decision, apply the materiality srandards inlRegularion i gios. t(u),
Regulation 18705.3(a), or Regulation 1870i.4(a)1, respectivef. If a business entity,
source of income, or source of a gift is not direcily involved in a governmental
decision, applylhe materiarity st_a1da$s in [Reguiation lg705.r(J), Regulation
I 8705.3(b), or Regulation I g705.4(b)1, resplcti-vely.,,
Because neither the business entity nor the soLlrce of income to Mayor Abed will be
initiating the proceeding or a named party in the proceeding, both of these economic interests are
indirectly involved.
SrBp 5: MarnnrAlrry SraNpann
A conflict of interest arises only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material, (Regulation
18700(a).) 
rs'' \r\v
Real Pronertv:
For indirectly involved real property, Regulation 18705.2(b)(1) provides that the financial
effect of a governmental decision on real piopert"y i, pr"rr.n"d not to be material. ..This
presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the
govemment decision, its financial effect, and the natuie of the real property in which the public
official has an economic interest, which makes it reasonable foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect of the real property in *rri"r, the public official has an interest.,,
Examples of circumstances that include detisionsihat:
"(A) The develo_pment potential or income producing potential of the real
property in which the official has an economic interest;
(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an economic
interest: and
(C) The character of the neighborhood including... subsrantial effects on:
traffic, view' privacy, intensity of uie, noise levels, air emissions, or similar traits
of the nei ghborhood. " (Regul ation I S7 O 5 .Z(b)( I XA_C). )


File No. A-10-l7l
Page No. 6
Generally, when a public official's property is located more than 500 feet from the
property that is the subject of the governmental detision, we will conclude that there will be no
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the official's property. Ho*"r"r, when the
magnitude of the change brought about by the governmental decision is such that it is clear that
there may be some affects on properti"r b"yonJ 500 feet, we must consider those affects.
A Triple-A Minor League Ballpark _could bring changes that would certainly implicate
the circumstances, listed in Regulation tgzos.z(bxlXt), as well as others, that may be used to
rebut the presumption that there will be no marerial affect.6 Th";";;;;iy'i1r be some impact
because of more traffic in the area surrounding the park and the noise from the park. The impact
is lessened because the surrounding properties all oip"o, to be commercial and industrial.
Furthermore, the impact from the park ii likely tme ior a great many properties surrounding the
site, with some properties being affected to a greater degree than others.
when the commission established the 500 foot mle it was intended to serve, as much as
possible, as a brighrline test for determining when the law will consider a financial effect on real
property to be material. Within 500 feet, even a one-penny effect is material. Beyond 500 feet
and the financial effect will not be considered material unless there are specific circumstances
under which the official property will receive a benefit or suffer a detriment substantially
disproportionate to other properties affected by the decision.
You have provided a map and other information regarding the site of the proposed
ballpark and its proximity to the Abed properties. It does not appear that the Abed properties
will be significantly impacted by ingress oregress to the proposed ballpark site. The properties
are commercial properties surrounded by other comm"."iol and/or inaustriaifroperties and,
therefore, are unlikely to be impacted by noise or privacy issues or other changes to the ,.traits of
the neighborhood." In short, on the baiis of information provided ,t,rrir p.ir", in time, it does
not appear that the presumption of nonmateriality has been rebutted. Accordingly, with respect
to the real property economic interest, Mayor Abed does not have a conflict of interest
participating in the referenced decision.
For business entities and sources of income that are business entities that are indirectly
involved the materiality standards are set forth in Regulation 18905.1(c). The dollar thresholds
for materiality vary according to the size of the busin-ess. you have not provided any
information regarding the revenues of either Mayor Abed business or that of his source of
income but assuming that they are both relatively small businesses 1n"t y.Jy in"o-" of under
$750,000), the materiality standards are set forth in Regulation tgz05.l 'rclfiland 
stare that rhe
financial effect of the governmental decision is materia'i if it is reasonably ftieseeable that:
u Because the com.mission has not provided a threshold fbr what is material with respect to indirectly
involved real property, we do not have any dlllar threshold tor aeciaing what is material.


File No. A-lO-177
Page No. 7
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or
more; or
(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 o, -ore; o,
(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value
of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more.
Srrp 6: RresoNanly FoRESEEABLE
Whether the financial consequences of a governmental decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time the decision is made depeids on the facts surroundinj ihe decision. A
financial effect need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, iut it must be more
than a mere possibility. (ln re Thorner (1975) I Fppc ops. 19s.;
Ultimately'i, it ,,p to the public official to make the determination through a good faith
effort. (Hensley Advice Letter, No. A-07-1 13; MoockAdvice Letter, No. A-01-l 40; O,Harra
Advice Letter, No. A-00-174.)

Panttcrp,ltloN
You have not given any facts indicating that either the "public generally,, exception or
that the "legally required participation" excepti,on would apply.
If you have any questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
C

Senior Counsel, Legal Division

because of more traffic in the area surrounding the park and the noise from the park. The impact
is lessened because the surrounding properties all oip"o, to be commercial and industrial.
Furthermore, the impact from the park ii likely tme ior a great many properties surrounding the
site, with some properties being affected to a greater degree than others.
when the commission established the 500 foot mle it was intended to serve, as much as
possible, as a brighrline test for determining when the law will consider a financial effect on real
property to be material. Within 500 feet, even a one-penny effect is material. Beyond 500 feet
and the financial effect will not be considered material unless there are specific circumstances
under which the official property will receive a benefit or suffer a detriment substantially
disproportionate to other properties affected by the decision.
You have provided a map and other information regarding the site of the proposed
ballpark and its proximity to the Abed properties. It does not appear that the Abed properties
will be significantly impacted by ingress oregress to the proposed ballpark site. The properties
are commercial properties surrounded by other comm"."iol and/or inaustriaifroperties and,
therefore, are unlikely to be impacted by noise or privacy issues or other changes to the ,.traits of
the neighborhood." In short, on the baiis of information provided ,t,rrir p.ir", in time, it does
not appear that the presumption of nonmateriality has been rebutted. Accordingly, with respect
to the real property economic interest, Mayor Abed does not have a conflict of interest
participating in the referenced decision.
For business entities and sources of income that are business entities that are indirectly
involved the materiality standards are set forth in Regulation 18905.1(c). The dollar thresholds
for materiality vary according to the size of the busin-ess. you have not provided any
information regarding the revenues of either Mayor Abed business or that of his source of
income but assuming that they are both relatively small businesses 1n"t y.Jy in"o-" of under
$750,000), the materiality standards are set forth in Regulation tgz05.l 'rclfiland

"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or
more; or
(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a

Whether the financial consequences of a governmental decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time the decision is made depeids on the facts surroundinj ihe decision. A
financial effect need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, iut it must be more
than a mere possibility. (ln re Thorner (1975) I Fppc ops. 19s.;
Ultimately'i, it ,,p to the public official to make the determination through a good faith
effort. (Hensley Advice Letter, No. A-07-1 13; MoockAdvice Letter, No. A-01-l 40; O,Harra
Advice Letter, No. A-00-174.)

QUESTION: 1. May Mayor Abed participate in the city council and/or Community Development
Commission's consideration of a governmental decision to acquire land and constrlrct a minor
league ballpark?

CONCLUSION: 1. Mayor Abed is presumed not to have a conflict of interest in the governmental
decision because his real property is located more than 500 feet from the property that is the
subject of the govemmental decision. However, this presumption may be rebutted if there are
factors that indicate there will be a material financial effect on Mayor Abed's property, as
discussed below.

McCain Ass istant City Attorney City of Escondido 201 North Broadway Escondido, California 92026 RE: Your Request for Advice Our File No. ,4.-10-177 Dear Ms. McCain: This letter responds to yoLlr request for advice on behalf of Escondido City Mayor Sam Adbed regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") I and is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Additionally, we base our advice solely on the provisions of the Act and do not adclress the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws, such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. QUESTION 1. May Mayor Abed participate in the city council and/or Community Development Commission's consideration of a governmental decision to acquire land and constrlrct a minor league ballpark? CONCLUSION 1. Mayor Abed is presumed not to have a conflict of interest in the governmental decision because his real property is located more than 500 feet from the property that is the subject of the govemmental decision. However, this presumption may be rebutted if there are factors that indicate there will be a material financial effect on Mayor Abed's property, as discussed below. ' The Political Retbrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections ttl000 rhrough 91014. All statutory ret'erences are to the GovernmentC<lde, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political practices Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through 1U997 of Title 2 of the Califirrnia Cgde of Regulati.ns. All regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Calitbrnia Code <tf Regulations. unless gtherwise indicated. File No. A-10-177 Page No. 2 FACTS Mayor Abed owns real property located at 540 and 562 W. Grand Avenue. Mayor Abed receives rental income from the property located at 562 W. Grand Avenue. He leases tire property to Tom and Judy Fitzgerald. Tom and Judy Fitzgerald own CrossFit Xystum LLC2 and have two years remaining on their lease. Tom and Judy Fitzgerald pay $3,100 per month to Mayor Abed. The property at 540 W. Grand is vacant. The governmental decision facing the Escondido City Council involves acquiring land and constntcting a minor league ballpark. Specifically, the City will be acquiring two parcels of land and using a portion of its own property to constnrct a minor league baflpark that will be leased to a minor league baseball team. The parcels of property ar" adiocerf to each other and consist of the "Spruce Street property," located at 480 North Spruce Street consisting of 3.7g acres; the North County Transit District property, consisting of 1.68 acres; and the eiisting City public works yard consisting of 7.82 acres.
